Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but it rarely worked out that way. I have about 15 hours a week that I dedicate to studying, but they're spread out inconsistently. Some weeks I'd binge study, other weeks I'd barely touch anything. I know I should be more organized, especially with 18 courses to manage, but I just... drift through it.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I'd have stretches where I wouldn't do much at all, then suddenly realize a deadline was approaching and scramble. Regular study times feel like a nice idea in theory, but my motivation comes in waves. When I'm feeling low, which happens more than I'd like to admit, maintaining any kind of schedule feels impossible.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or guilt that made me log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post or an assignment, and that would prompt me. Other times I'd log in just to check if I'd missed something important, scroll around for a bit, then log out without really accomplishing much. It wasn't really a habit so much as a reaction to external pressure.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a natural routine for me. There were brief periods where I logged in more regularly, usually when a peer study group I was part of was active and we'd coordinate through the platform. That peer support helped for a while, but once the group activity died down, so did my consistent engagement. It felt more like something I had to do rather than wanted to do.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me since I learn best through reading and writing. I appreciated well-organized lecture notes and any supplementary articles. When something was clearly written and I could download it, I'd actually engage with it deeply on my own time. The videos were okay, but I'd often just skim through them.
Q6: I definitely ignored the discussion forums more than I should have. I posted occasionally, but I rarely felt like my contributions mattered or that the discussions went anywhere meaningful. I also delayed watching videos until the last minute, and even then I'd watch them at 2x speed just to get through them. They felt like a chore more than a learning opportunity.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my grades or when it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. If something felt like busy work or if the instructions were unclear, I'd lose interest quickly. I need to feel like there's a point to what I'm doing, otherwise my motivation just evaporates.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. I'd tell myself I should do them because they might help, but then I'd never actually prioritize them. Required activities at least had consequences attached, so I'd force myself through them. Optional ones would sit in my mental to-do list, making me feel guilty, but I rarely got to them. The lack of external pressure meant my internal motivation wasn't enough.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. A lot of my actual learning happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then study them on my tablet without being connected. I have notebooks filled with handwritten summaries and notes that I'd work through on my own. I also used some external resources like YouTube videos and academic articles I found through Google Scholar. None of that shows up anywhere.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing downloaded materials or rewriting my notes to better understand concepts. I'd be sitting in a coffee shop, deeply engaged with course content, but the LMS would show nothing. My best studying often happened when I was completely disconnected from the platform, just me and my notes. The LMS only captures when I'm online, not when I'm actually learning.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty much my standard approach. I'd log in once, download everything I needed for a module, and then work through it offline over several days. Sometimes I wouldn't log back in until the next assignment was due. It was more efficient for me to have everything saved locally where I could annotate and organize it my way.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging felt almost impossible. When I was feeling really sad or anxious, even logging in felt overwhelming. Around midterms, when everything piled up at once, I just shut down for a bit. Personal stuff was also happening in my life that made it hard to focus on academics. Those were the times my attendance and engagement really dropped.
Q13: Fatigue and competing responsibilities were huge factors. I'm 26, so I have life obligations beyond just being a student. When I was exhausted or stressed about other things, the LMS became something I avoided rather than engaged with. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but 'later' kept getting pushed back. The platform felt like another demand on my limited energy.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind would be completely elsewhere. I'd watch a video and realize at the end that I hadn't absorbed anything. Sometimes I'd click through readings just to mark them as complete without actually processing the content. It looked like engagement, but it was really just going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was anxiety – the bigger the task, the more I'd avoid it. Part of it was just feeling disconnected from the course and questioning why any of it mattered. I'd distract myself with other things, then panic when deadlines loomed. It's a frustrating cycle I haven't figured out how to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that reliably got me to engage. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. I'd often log in the day before something was due and cram everything in. My LMS activity probably shows these spikes right before due dates. It's not healthy or ideal, but that external pressure was the main force driving my participation.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. When I got encouraging feedback, it would boost my motivation briefly, and I'd engage more for a week or so. But if feedback was delayed or felt generic, it reinforced my sense that my efforts didn't really matter. The peer support intervention helped because hearing from classmates felt more immediate and personal than waiting for instructor comments.
Q18: The structure of the course influenced me more than I expected. When modules were clearly laid out with specific deadlines, I could at least plan around them. But when things were vague or there was too much flexibility, I'd lose track completely. Having 18 courses meant I needed structure to keep everything straight, and courses without it suffered in my attention.
Q19: Courses with well-organized reading materials and clear expectations made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when there were too many different platforms or tools to keep track of, or when instructions were buried in long videos I had to watch to find key information. I also struggled when discussion participation felt forced rather than organic.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying to figure things out or feeling anxious about performance. They might be exploring resources, re-reading materials, or just clicking around trying to understand what's expected. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes it's just overwhelmed students searching for clarity they can't find.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My exam scores and assignment quality are high when I actually submit work, but my LMS activity is low because I do most of my real learning offline. I'm a strong reader and writer, so I can engage deeply with materials on my own without needing to be constantly logged in. The platform doesn't capture that.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when students log in, what they click on, and whether they submit work on time. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the emotional state of the student, and whether someone is actually learning or just going through motions. They capture behavior but not understanding or genuine connection to the material.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what I'm learning and feeling connected to the course in some meaningful way. It's not just about logging in or completing tasks – it's about being mentally present and curious. For me, real engagement happens when I'm genuinely interested and can see how the material matters. That's been hard to maintain this term.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally this term, or how the peer support intervention actually helped me feel less alone even if it didn't dramatically change my login patterns. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with downloaded materials, or the quality of understanding I developed despite my low visible activity. It misses the whole human experience of being a student.
